_meta.description: Roadmap refreshed after reviewing the more mature ../vermilion project structure and full commit history. The old phase of RAM-address naming and loc_ cleanup is effectively complete; the remaining gap to vermilion is architectural maturity: split modules under src/, split constant files, stronger docs/tooling, and a systematic audit for hidden pointers inside data blocks.
_meta.source_project_reviewed: ../vermilion (structure, docs, todos.json, roadmap/refactor docs, and full git commit subjects)
_meta.label_progress: 0 active loc_ label definitions remain in the build. Remaining naming work is consistency/audit of opaque data blocks, not routine/branch label cleanup.
_meta.ram_progress: All known work RAM, I/O, and core hardware addresses are now symbolized. Remaining constant work is architectural reorganization, not broad raw-address cleanup.
current_state: Single active source file: smgp.asm still contains the whole ROM in build order
Bit-perfect verification exists and is the required correctness gate via verify.bat
Startup, race loop, driving model, rendering/HUD, race flow, Track_data, slope data, and sign data are already documented
Major helper macros already exist for VDP/control-flow patterns; future macro work is selective cleanup, not first-wave adoption
The largest parity gaps versus vermilion are include-based source splitting, split constants files, symbol/index tooling, module-level docs, and a hidden-pointer audit of opaque data blocks
Open reverse-engineering focus areas now center on AI/placement internals, Z80/audio internals, artwork/blob identification, and pointer-like values still buried in data
completed_milestones: Bit-perfect Windows-first build/verify workflow is in place
All known loc_ label definitions have been renamed
All known RAM/I/O address references have symbolic names
Track_data, slope-data encoding, and sign-data format have been decoded
Core gameplay, rendering, and race-flow routines have headers/comments

acceptance: No code or data move changes ROM bytes or label addresses unless the result still verifies bit-perfect
New labels follow repo conventions and preserve original ROM address comments when renaming legacy symbols
New constants live in the appropriate split constants file once ARCH-005 is complete
editing_rules: Prefer symbolic constants over raw RAM, I/O, and hardware literals
Prefer existing proven macros over repeating exact instruction sequences
Preserve ROM order exactly while splitting the monolith into include files
Use Python 3 standard library for automation unless a stronger repo-local convention emerges

always: Run cmd //c verify.bat after every code or data change
If verify fails, isolate the exact cause rather than reverting unrelated work
When splitting files, compare listing addresses before and after each extraction step
before_refactors: Add or preserve routine headers and invariants before moving code into a new module
Confirm all dc.b/dc.w/dc.l blocks remain byte-identical after formatting or symbolization
Preserve original ROM-offset comments for renamed legacy symbols and pointer blocks
Keep a referenceable monolithic source or generated address map while the split is in progress

architecture_and_split.description: Primary parity gap with vermilion. The next major milestone is to turn the monolithic build into a thin top-level file plus ordered src/ modules without changing ROM output.

tasks.title: Split smgp.asm into a thin top-level include file
tasks.description: Make smgp.asm behave like vermilion.asm: a short file that includes macros/constants plus ordered modules under src/. This is the immediate next step and the biggest maintainability win still missing.
tasks.progress_notes: Initial split complete and bit-perfect verified. `smgp.asm` is now a 5-line top-level include file loading `macros.asm`, `constants.asm`, `header.asm`, `init.asm`, and `src/core.asm`. This establishes the include-based build shape needed for further module extraction without changing ROM output.

tasks.title: Extract header, vectors, error traps, and startup into dedicated files
tasks.description: Create low-risk first modules similar to vermilion's header.asm and init.asm so top-of-ROM structures stop living inside the gameplay monolith.
tasks.progress_notes: First low-risk extraction step complete and bit-perfect verified. Top-of-ROM structures now live in `header.asm` (vectors, ROM header, error traps) and `init.asm` (EntryPoint, VBlank/HBlank setup, boot helpers, startup graphics/Z80 loader, Boot_init_data, Initialize_vdp).

tasks.title: Split gameplay code into thematic src modules
tasks.description: Break the remaining code region into modules such as core, race flow, driving model, rendering/HUD, menus, AI, and audio interface, preserving exact ROM order and using the existing documentation as boundaries.
tasks.progress_notes: Eleventh extraction step complete and bit-perfect verified. The late-game/result path now has its own `src/endgame.asm`, pulling the results-screen setup, credits/car-spec endgame flow, and closely-related late-game data away from the residual `src/gameplay.asm` tail. `src/gameplay.asm` now starts at `Draw_track_name_and_championship_standings`, so the remaining module is mostly championship/race-summary glue plus shared late-game helpers. New insight: the source split is now mature enough that additional architecture work should focus on stable data-region extraction (`ARCH-004`) rather than more tiny code-only splits.

tasks.title: Split major data tables and asset regions into dedicated modules
tasks.description: Move track/team/text/graphics/sound regions into ordered src/*.asm or data-oriented include files once the code split is stable. This prepares the project for pointer auditing and later extraction tooling.
tasks.progress_notes: All major data region extractions from `src/gameplay.asm` are now complete and bit-perfect verified. Over the full run, 20+ extraction steps have landed. Data modules extracted this session: `src/team_messages_data.asm` (1,564 lines: all JP/EN team message pointer tables and strings), `src/crash_gauge_data.asm` (329 lines: `Race_hud_tiles_g` + crash RPM/sync/collision gauge palette tables and streams), `src/car_sprite_blobs.asm` (4,801 lines: `Startup_tileset_data` through `Car_sprite_data_53F50`), `src/hud_and_minimap_data.asm` (426 lines: `Race_hud_car_tiles` through per-track minimap tiles/maps), `src/screen_art_data.asm` (1,911 lines: `Hud_tiles_normal` through `Championship_final_tiles`), `src/track_bg_data.asm` (1,719 lines: all 19 `Track_bg_tilemap_*` and `Track_bg_tiles_*` families), `src/road_and_track_data.asm` (2,980 lines: `Ai_screen_y_table`, `Road_scanline_descriptor_table`, `Road_displacement_*`, `Sign_lookup_table`/sequences, and all per-track `*_curve_data`, `*_slope_data`, `*_phys_slope_data`, `*_minimap_pos`, `*_sign_data`, `*_sign_tileset` for all 19 tracks), `src/audio_engine.asm` (1,848 lines: `Halt_audio_sequence` through `Z80_data` — the full 68K audio engine and Z80 ROM payload). `src/gameplay.asm` now contains only the core gameplay code (9,638 lines) with all data regions extracted as include files. Previously extracted in prior sessions: `src/track_config_data.asm`, `src/sprite_frame_data.asm`, and 10+ endgame/result/portrait data modules. The monolith split is effectively complete; remaining work under ARCH-004 would be further granularity within the existing data modules (e.g. splitting `car_sprite_blobs.asm` or `screen_art_data.asm` by asset family), which is lower priority than the documentation and tooling tasks.

tasks.title: Split constants.asm into hardware, RAM, game, and sound constant files
tasks.description: Match vermilion's mature layout by separating constants into focused files such as hw_constants.asm, ram_addresses.asm, game_constants.asm, and sound_constants.asm, then wire them into the new top-level build.
tasks.progress_notes: Initial constants split complete and bit-perfect verified. `constants.asm` is now a thin include file that pulls in `hw_constants.asm`, `ram_addresses.asm`, `sound_constants.asm`, and `game_constants.asm`. `tools/split_constants.py` was added to regenerate the split files from the monolithic baseline while the architecture migration continues.

tasks.title: Create module-boundary and refactor-log docs for the split build
tasks.description: Add docs/module_boundaries.md and docs/refactor_log.md so future contributors can see ownership, call boundaries, known misplacements, and why each extraction step was safe. This is a major part of vermilion's mature shape.

tasks.title: Preserve a reference-only monolithic source during the split
tasks.description: Keep one reference file or generated address map that represents the pre-split monolith so line/address comparisons remain easy while modules are being extracted.
tasks.progress_notes: Added `smgp_full.asm` as a generated reference-only monolithic snapshot composed from the current include tree. It is not part of the build, but it preserves a single-file view for line-based inspection during the ongoing split.

documentation_and_data_audit.description: Once the split exists, the next parity step is the same one vermilion went through: module-level docs plus systematic auditing of opaque data and embedded pointers.

tasks.title: Document AI car behavior, placement logic, and standings internals
tasks.description: Identify the routines and data that govern AI speed, lane choice, placement score, sort order, and post-race standings updates. This is one of the largest remaining gameplay-understanding gaps.

tasks.title: Annotate remaining major data tables and asset lists entry-by-entry
tasks.description: Add per-entry labels/comments for team data, driver data, portraits, HUD assets, standings tables, championship data, sprite-frame tables, and other large named data regions so they become safely editable without debugger context.

tasks.title: Build a hidden-pointer scanner for data blocks
tasks.description: Create a tool that scans listing output and assembly data blocks for values that resolve inside the ROM, RAM, or structured tables but are not yet labeled as pointers. This is the explicit next step after the src split.
tasks.progress_notes: Added `tools/report_hidden_pointers.py` and generated `tools/index/hidden_pointer_candidates.json`. The scan was refined repeatedly: from an initial 270 noisy `dc.l`/`dc.w` hits, to 22 candidates / 11 strong `dc.l` candidates, to 4 reviewed candidates, and finally to 0 remaining candidates after auditing and suppressing known false-positive classes. The tool now also has first-pass support for hidden addresses encoded across tuple-style `dc.b` and `dc.w` data, but the naive tuple sweep produced overwhelming bitmap/layout noise, so the current tuple heuristic is intentionally constrained and conservative. Insight: real hidden RAM-pointer tables were found in result/podium data and symbolized; broader tuple-pointer discovery will need domain-specific structure heuristics rather than blind sliding-window matching.

tasks.title: Audit candidate hidden pointers and convert confirmed ones to labeled tables
tasks.description: Review PTR-001 output, confirm which candidates are real pointers or dispatch tables, and rewrite those regions as named dc.l/dc.w tables or mixed-format structures with comments.
tasks.progress_notes: Confirmed and converted two real hidden pointer tables in `src/gameplay.asm`: `Race_finish_lap_time_ptrs` / `Arcade_car_spec_stat_ptrs` now use `Lap_time_table_ptr+4`, `Lap_time_table_ptr+8`, and `Rival_race_time_bcd`; `Race_finish_buffer_ptr_table` now uses symbolic `Tilemap_work_buf` offsets instead of raw RAM addresses. Follow-up audit cleared the remaining direct-number scanner hits as false positives: `Boot_init_data` is intentional boot metadata, `Vdp_init_register_table` and `Track_data` contain scalar/control values, and the `Speed_to_distance_table` match is numeric coincidence inside a monotonic fixed-point table. New insight: tuple-encoded pointers definitely remain plausible in `dc.b`/`dc.w` structures, but extracting them reliably will require label-specific and format-specific heuristics under PTR-003 rather than a generic sliding-window scan.

tasks.title: Identify mixed code/data and implicit dispatch structures revealed by the split
tasks.description: Use module boundaries plus pointer-candidate results to find code-in-data blobs, inline command streams, and address-like records that still need disassembly or structure comments.
tasks.progress_notes: Five focused `smgp.lst`-guided mixed-structure audits have landed. First, `Credits_car_frame_table` in `src/gameplay.asm` was confirmed to be a 3-entry hidden pointer table encoded as raw longwords targeting six embedded sprite-record starts inside the blob immediately before `Credits_tiles`; those targets are now labeled `Credits_car_frame_a` through `Credits_car_frame_f`, the table now uses symbolic `dc.l` entries, and the dynamic frame swaps in `Credits_car_obj_update` use labels instead of raw ROM addresses. Second, `Arcade_car_spec_car_positions` was confirmed to be a 4-record mixed structure of `y.w, x.w, sprite_frame_ptr.l`, with the raw longword pointers targeting four embedded sprite records inside `Arcade_car_spec_palette_stream`; those records are now labeled `Arcade_car_spec_car_frame_a` through `Arcade_car_spec_car_frame_d`. Third, the AI car init records in `src/objects.asm` were audited against `Init_ai_car_fields` and `smgp.lst`: they are confirmed 20-byte mixed records whose third long is not a direct sprite-data pointer but a pointer into `Car_sprite_ptr_table`, while the surrounding longs/words are scalar setup fields. Fourth, `Endgame_car_anim_tile_table` was checked against its consumer and `smgp.lst`: the apparent `$87xx` 'pointers' are just tile IDs copied straight to a VDP tilemap buffer, and the old `Update_tire_wear_counter_Arcade-1` expression was only being used to materialize literal tile word `$87DD`, not to reference executable code. Fifth, the crash RPM/sync gauge tables were confirmed to be 8-byte mixed records indexed by gauge state: 3 threshold/state bytes followed by a pointer to a palette-write stream consumed by `Write_3_palette_vdp_bytes`, while the pointed-to streams themselves are sprite/tile tuple data rather than code or dispatch tables. Key insight: listing-guided, consumer-first audits are just as valuable for ruling out false pointer interpretations as they are for confirming real embedded dispatch/frame structures. Sixth and final: all remaining raw ROM pointer literals in src/objects.asm were replaced with symbolic labels: #$00010920 -> #Crash_car_spin_behind_sprite_frames, #$0006E894 -> #Ai_angle_table, #$00003800 -> #Arcade_race_init, #$0000D6E2 -> #Championship_standings_init (2 sites), #$0000D2B0 -> #Team_select_screen_init; plus two pre-existing bugs were fixed (missing instructions in Crash_car_obj2_init and Championship_next_race_init). Also fixed: missing dc.l Sprite_frame_data_12086 entry in Crash_depth_sprite_frames table (src/sprite_frame_data.asm). Build is bit-perfect.

tasks.title: Identify and name stable artwork/blob boundaries for future extraction
tasks.description: Catalog which graphics, palette, and sound-adjacent blobs are clearly bounded and understood well enough to label or eventually move behind incbin without risking hidden internal pointers.

audio_and_tooling.description: Vermilion's maturity comes partly from project tooling and partly from isolating tricky subsystems like sound. smgp needs both.

tasks.title: Document the 68K-to-Z80 sound interface and command flow
tasks.description: Map the command queue, bus handoff, sound-driver RAM window, and known music/SFX command semantics so the audio boundary is readable even before a full Z80 disassembly exists.

tasks.title: Isolate and name Z80 driver tables and opaque sound data blocks
tasks.description: After the sound interface is documented, identify driver-local tables, command streams, and stable data boundaries so the audio region can be split and prepared for later disassembly or extraction.

tasks.title: Export a machine-readable symbol map from smgp.lst
tasks.description: Create a tool that emits label-to-ROM-address data for every symbol in the build. This should become the backbone for address comparisons, pointer scans, editor tooling, and future docs.
tasks.progress_notes: Added `tools/index/symbol_map.py`. It parses `smgp.lst` and emits `tools/index/symbol_map.json` with 4221 labels mapped to ROM addresses. This now serves as the baseline for split-safety checks and future pointer-audit tooling.

tasks.title: Add a run_checks aggregator plus core structural linters
tasks.description: Create tools/run_checks.py and supporting linters for raw-address regressions, reintroduced loc_ labels, include-order drift, and other hygiene checks that matter once the build is split across many files.
tasks.progress_notes: Added `tools/run_checks.py`. It currently verifies top-level include order, validates `tools/index/symbol_map.json`, rejects new `loc_` label definitions, runs the split-address checker, and performs a raw-address sweep with an explicit per-file allowlist for known unresolved literals and intentional magic values. Current result: all structural checks pass on the split tree.

tasks.title: Add comment-density and function-header coverage reporting
tasks.description: Mirror vermilion's documentation-driven cleanup by generating per-file comment-density and header-coverage reports after the source is split into modules.

tasks.title: Add a split-safety checker that compares symbol addresses before and after moves
tasks.description: Before and during module extraction, compare the pre-split and post-split listing outputs to prove that labels stay at the same ROM addresses and that no include ordering mistake changed bytes.
tasks.progress_notes: Added `tools/check_split_addresses.py`. It compares the current `smgp.lst` against the baseline symbol map and reports missing, moved, or extra labels. Current result after the first split: all 4221 baseline symbols still match their original addresses exactly.

tasks.title: Create machine-readable extraction and injection foundations for named data
tasks.description: Build the same kind of tooling foundation vermilion uses for randomizer/editor work: export named track, team, text, standings, and related data to structured files and support round-tripping back to assembly safely.

modding_foundation.description: Longer-term parity with vermilion means the disassembly should support real editing/randomization tools once the split and pointer audit are solid.

tasks.title: Write a modding and data-roundtrip architecture document
tasks.description: Define which extracted formats map back to assembly, where checksum/update steps fit, how verify.bat remains the final gate, and which data classes are safe to edit automatically.

tasks.title: Build a track editor CLI
tasks.description: Create a tool that edits structured track records, slope data, sign placement, and related references, then writes the results back safely through the extraction/injection layer.

tasks.title: Build a team, driver, and car-stats editor CLI
tasks.description: Create an editor for team ordering, driver mappings, portraits, engine tables, acceleration tables, and team-specific characteristics once those modules and tables are stable.

tasks.title: Build a text and sign-message editor CLI
tasks.description: Create an editor for text tables, team messages, sign content, and language-dependent strings using the split text/data modules and any format constraints documented during the pointer/data audit.

tasks.title: Build a championship and progression editor CLI
tasks.description: Create an editor for standings, points awards, race order, progression flags, and championship flow once AI/placement and progression tables are fully documented.

regression_and_notes.description: Keep the project trustworthy while the architecture changes and convert notes into a maintained index rather than a scratchpad.

tasks.title: Add pre-commit hook scripts for Windows and Bash
tasks.description: Provide simple hook installers that run verify.bat and tools/run_checks.py automatically, mirroring vermilion's later regression-prevention work.

tasks.title: Add targeted lints for hidden-pointer regressions and raw-symbol backsliding
tasks.description: Flag newly introduced raw RAM/I/O literals, any accidental reintroduction of loc_-style labels, and pointer-like values added to cleaned data regions without documentation.

tasks.title: Keep notes.txt as a live open-problems index mapped to split modules
tasks.description: After the split, rewrite notes.txt so unresolved topics point at module paths and named labels instead of monolithic line-of-thought notes.

tasks.title: Maintain docs/refactor_log.md as module and pointer work lands
tasks.description: Record major label, module, macro, and pointer-audit milestones in a human-readable log so the path from monolith to mature layout stays searchable outside commit history.

priority_order: --- Immediate parity work: make the build look like vermilion structurally ---
--- Next: audit opaque data for hidden pointers and mixed structures ---
--- Then: fill the biggest remaining understanding gaps ---
--- Then: harden the project with vermilion-style tooling/docs ---
